absorptivity.sourceName: Absorption of 1.06 μm laser radiation by stainless steel

laserPower.description: Optimal average power for metal oxide removal

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength for optimal metal absorption

spotSize.description: Beam diameter range for cleaning; smaller for fine details (50-100 μm), larger for broad areas (200-300 μm). 150 μm recommended for SS304 industrial prep to ensure even fluence distribution. Source: Equipment specs from Adapt Laser Systems and Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2019).

frequency.description: Typical for 1064 nm cleaning to achieve uniform energy delivery; higher rates (>50 kHz) speed up process but risk heat buildup on reflective SS304. 30 kHz recommended for balance. Trade-off: faster cleaning vs. potential oxidation. Source: Academic literature in Lasers in Engineering (2021).

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective industrial cleaning of SS304; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) reduce HAZ but are less common. 30 ns minimizes thermal effects while ablating contaminants. Source: Laser physics research in Optics Express (2017) on ablation thresholds for metals.

scanSpeed.description: Effective range for contaminant removal without substrate ablation; slower for heavy contamination (e.g., 500-1000 mm/s), faster for light (2000-3000 mm/s). Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal balance on SS304 to minimize heat accumulation due to its moderate thermal conductivity (16 W/mK). Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals and Applied Surface Science (2020).

passCount.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (up to 5) for heavy layers; 2 passes recommended for complete removal on SS304 without excessive heat input. Source: Industry case studies in Journal of Cleaner Production (2022) on metal surface preparation.

overlapRatio.description: Ensures uniform coverage and avoids gaps/striping; 60% recommended for SS304 cleaning to optimize energy overlap without overexposure. Typical 30-80% based on scan patterns. Source: Process optimization in Applied Physics A (2020).
